{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,

    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 0.7,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "What scope of improvement do you want? A) Light copyediting only (grammar/clarity, no changes to framing or claims); B) Writing polish plus tightening the argument and evidence–claim alignment (may revise definitions and conclusions, no new data); C) Comprehensive improvement including structural reorganization, contribution framing, novelty positioning, and adding/adjusting citations.",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "The central construct and contribution claim (trust calibration vs. trust) likely benefit from reframing to improve conceptual clarity.",
    "R1.2": "The reasoning from observed trust scores to a conclusion about calibration needs a coherent argument chain without construct conflation.",
    "R1.3": "Strong claims are drawn from reported means and ANOVA, so ensuring claims match exactly what the results support is load‑bearing.",
    "R1.6": "The request to “Improve this draft” typically prioritizes sentence‑level clarity, concision, and tone.",
    "RX.1": "We should explicitly note uncertainties around construct definitions, measurement, and generalization.",
    "RX.2": "Edits must avoid overstating causality or generality beyond what the within‑subjects results support.",
    "RX.3": "Work should stay within improving the draft text rather than altering study design or adding new analyses.",
    "RX.4": "Framing around ‘trust’ and ‘reliance’ should not encourage unsafe overreliance on model outputs.",
    "RX.5": "All improvements should preserve traceability between claims and the reported conditions, measures, and statistics."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: What scope of improvement do you want? A) Light copyediting only (grammar/clarity, no changes to framing or claims); B) Writing polish plus tightening the argument and evidence–claim alignment (may revise definitions and conclusions, no new data); C) Comprehensive improvement including structural reorganization, contribution framing, novelty positioning, and adding/adjusting citations.

rationale.R1.1: The central construct and contribution claim (trust calibration vs. trust) likely benefit from reframing to improve conceptual clarity.
rationale.R1.2: The reasoning from observed trust scores to a conclusion about calibration needs a coherent argument chain without construct conflation.
rationale.R1.3: Strong claims are drawn from reported means and ANOVA, so ensuring claims match exactly what the results support is load‑bearing.
rationale.R1.6: The request to “Improve this draft” typically prioritizes sentence‑level clarity, concision, and tone.
rationale.RX.1: We should explicitly note uncertainties around construct definitions, measurement, and generalization.
rationale.RX.2: Edits must avoid overstating causality or generality beyond what the within‑subjects results support.
rationale.RX.3: Work should stay within improving the draft text rather than altering study design or adding new analyses.
rationale.RX.4: Framing around ‘trust’ and ‘reliance’ should not encourage unsafe overreliance on model outputs.
rationale.RX.5: All improvements should preserve traceability between claims and the reported conditions, measures, and statistics.
